Section 16.11
OXIDATIVE CLEAVAGE OF VICINAL DIOLS
Copyright © 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Oxidative Cleavage of Vicinal Diols
Vicinal (1,2) diols undergo oxidation—loss of H 2 —and rearrangement to form two separated aldehydes or ketones
The oxidant is periodic acid ( HIO 4 )

Thiols: Sulfur Analogues of Alcohols
Thiols (mercaptans) contain the –SH group and resemble alcohols in structure and reactivity
The terrible odor of thiols is legendary
There are some important differences in reactivity between thiols and alcohols

Reactivity of Thiols
Thiols are considerably more acidic than alcohols and water
Naturally, thiol ate anions are less basic than alkoxides, but they are excellent nucleophiles toward C

Oxidation of Thiols
At first glance, oxidation of thiols should produce thiocarbonyl compounds
However, sulfur is a strong nucleophile toward oxygen— sulfoxides are the usual products of oxidation of thiols
Precursor to sulfonates!

Oxidation of Thiols-1
Oxidation can also produce a disulfide , the sulfur analogue of a peroxide ( RO 2 R cannot generally be prepared this way )
This type of oxidation is important in a biological context

Section 16.13
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF ALCOHOLS AND THIOLS

Infrared Spectroscopy
Alcohols give a highly characteristic, broad peak from 3200 – 3500 cm − 1 due to the O–H stretch
Thiols give a weak band from 2550 – 2700 cm − 1 due to the S–H stretch

1 H NMR Spectroscopy
The alcohol C–H proton appears between δ 3.3 – 4; the shift of the O–H proton is highly variable
C–H in thiols is not quite as deshielded as in alcohols :

Mass Spectrometry
Loss of water is common in mass spectra of alcohols; the molecular ion peak tends to be weak
Primary alcohols ( RCH 2 OH ) fragment to give protonated formaldehyde ( CH 2 OH + , m ∕ z =31 )
